title: Bass Note Spectra of Binary Forms
speaker: Giorgos Kotsovolis
abstract: In the 1940s Mahler initiated the program of determining the bass note spectrum Spec(P):={infx⎯⎯∈Λ∖0⎯⎯∣∣P(x⎯⎯)∣∣,Λ⊂ℝk a unimodular lattice} for some homogeneous form P. Understanding this spectrum is central in the geometry of numbers and offers a unified framework for many unsolved problems in number theory and homogeneous dynamics. However, not many cases are known. When P is a binary quadratic form, Spec(P) is well studied and is classically related to the Markoff spectrum. For binary forms of higher degrees much less is known. Mordell determined the extremal lattices for the spectra of binary cubic forms and further conjectured the existence of spectral gaps, a conjecture later disproved by Davenport. In this talk, we resolve this spectral problem for all binary cubic forms and more generally for all binary forms P of degree n≥3, by showing that quite contrary to these conjectures the spectrum Spec(P) is always an interval.

proof_stages.label: Bass-note spectrum as a lattice minimum
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines the differential operator attached to a homogeneous polynomial and identifies its first torus eigenvalue with the least value of the polynomial on the dual lattice, yielding Mahler's global spectrum and its basic boundedness, cusp, and maximum-attainment properties.

proof_stages.label: Benchmark spectral behaviors
proof_stages.summary: Several known higher-dimensional cases are reviewed to show that spectra can be intervals, sequences accumulating at zero, or conjecturally controlled by difficult diagonal-orbit dynamics.

proof_stages.label: Reduction to binary forms and low-degree baseline
proof_stages.summary: For two variables, varying the unimodular lattice is reformulated as moving the binary form in its SL2 orbit with fixed discriminant, after which linear and quadratic cases are classified, including the Markoff spectrum for indefinite quadratics.

proof_stages.label: Binary cubic interval theorem and proof template
proof_stages.summary: Mordell's extremal cubic forms and Davenport's disproof of spectral gaps motivate the theorem that cubic spectra are full intervals; the proof fixes or isolates roots, uses continued-fraction control of cubic approximation, obtains local stability on almost-full sets, and applies Steinhaus-type interval production.

proof_stages.label: Higher-degree theorem and almost-extremal strategy
proof_stages.summary: The main theorem states that every nonzero-discriminant binary n-ic form has interval spectrum, and the chief new obstacle is the absence of known extremal lattices, prompting the search for nearby almost-extremal lattices with good Diophantine properties.

proof_stages.label: Density-increment construction of good almost-extremal lattices
proof_stages.summary: The proof defines the sets of nearby numbers with no worse Diophantine behavior, proves a structural alternative saying either good points are already dense or become dense after zooming, and iterates this over all roots to produce almost-extremal lattices with exponent-two root data and hence no spectral gap.

proof_stages.label: Diagonal perturbations and thickened-flow interval filling
proof_stages.summary: Starting from the good almost-extremal lattices, diagonal perturbations reproduce the cubic local-stability argument up to a null set, and a thickened diagonal-flow/density-increment argument replaces Steinhaus when the root variables are dependent, yielding small intervals whose limit fills the whole spectrum.

examples.note: The integer lattice is used to make the eigenvalue/minimum dictionary visible by computing the exponential eigenfunctions explicitly before passing to arbitrary lattices.

examples.name: Sum of squares and triangular lattice packing
examples.note: The Euclidean norm-square form gives a continuous shortest-vector problem whose spectrum is an interval ending at the Hermite constant, with the binary case realized by the triangular lattice.
examples.engagement_reason: He specializes to the sum of squares, identifies the quantity as the squared shortest vector, argues continuity, and derives an interval spectrum with the triangular lattice as the 2D extremal case.

passes.reason: The sum-of-squares/Hermite example culminates in the triangular lattice, the symmetric densest two-dimensional lattice packing.
The sum-of-squares norm is rotationally regular and the triangular lattice is the Hermite-optimal packing lattice in dimension two.

The sum-of-squares/triangular-lattice example is maximally regular and the triangular lattice attains the two-dimensional Hermite packing optimum.

examples.name: Indefinite quadratic forms in at least three variables
examples.note: Oppenheim's theorem and the Hasse principle illustrate that some bass-note spectra are sequences, or even just zero, rather than intervals.
examples.engagement_reason: He applies Oppenheim/Margulis and the Hasse principle to indefinite quadratic forms to determine when the infimum is zero and describe the resulting spectrum.

examples.name: Markoff value from an integral indefinite quadratic form
examples.note: The displayed integral form of discriminant five, representing one but not zero, refutes the naive guess that all indefinite binary quadratic spectra vanish and leads into the Markoff spectrum.
examples.engagement_reason: He analyzes a specific integral indefinite quadratic form, shows it does not represent zero but represents one, computes its infimum, and obtains the Markoff value 1/sqrt(5).

passes.reason: The integral indefinite form gives the largest Markoff value 1/sqrt(5) and is explicitly used to refute the idea that all such infima vanish.
The integral indefinite quadratic form gives the largest Markoff value 1/sqrt(5) and is explicitly used to refute the guess that all such infima are zero.

The discriminant-5 integral indefinite form gives a nonzero Markoff value, refuting the guess that all such infima vanish, and its normalized value is the largest Markoff point.
The integral indefinite quadratic form gives the value 1/sqrt(5), explicitly refuting the all-zero intuition and in fact attaining the largest Markoff value.

examples.name: Fractal middle of the Markoff spectrum
examples.note: In response to a question, the speaker describes the region between Hall's interval and the Markoff-discrete part as containing intervals but also complicated fractal behavior related to continued fractions and closed geodesics.
examples.engagement_reason: He only describes the middle of the Markoff spectrum as having intervals and bizarre fractal phenomena without working through a specific construction or computation.

passes.reason: The middle Markoff spectrum is an identifiable fractal region described as complicated rather than as an exception or obstruction.
